title: Suminagashi, ink on water
subtitle: Concentric-ring marbling from the Japanese ink-on-water tradition
excerpt: Suminagashi, the Japanese ink-on-water marbling tradition dating from the Heian period, produces the characteristic concentric-ring patterns by dropping alternating drops of sumi ink and distilled water onto the surface of a shallow water bath. The rings spread and interlock; a sheet of mordanted paper is laid on the surface to lift the pattern.
sourceNotes: Suminagashi is documented as early as the Heian period (794 to 1185 AD) in Japanese court records; the technique and its name are public domain by age. The modern practice is synthesised from multiple historical and craft references, including the inkstone and brush traditions documented in Meiji-era craft publications (Internet Archive). C. W. Woolnough, The Art of Marbling (1853, 1881), mentions suminagashi as the earliest known marbling tradition.

content.text: Suminagashi means 'ink floating on water'. It is the oldest recorded marbling tradition, practised at the Heian court in Japan. European marbling that came later uses a thickened bath of carrageenan. The
method uses plain water. The ink sits on the water's surface tension, not on a thick 'size'. The patterns come out softer, with translucent edges and the
that are the signature of the craft.

attrs.body: Wear gloves when applying the alum mordant wash; sumi ink stains permanently, so do not eat or drink at the tray.
attrs.title: Marbling chemistry

items.name: Distilled water (for the drops that push the rings outward)
Marbling tray, at least A5 size
Paper, mordanted with alum solution, dried
Potassium aluminium sulphate (alum mordant) + water for mordanting paper

content.text: Mix the alum solution: one tablespoon of alum in 500 ml of warm water until dissolved. Wearing gloves, sponge the alum solution over the surface of each sheet and allow to dry completely.
sheets can be prepared a day in advance.

Fill the tray with plain tap water to a depth of 30 to 40 mm. The water should be still before beginning. Room temperature is fine; suminagashi does not require the controlled temperature that carrageenan sizing needs.

Load the eyedropper with sumi ink. Hold it just above the water surface, 5 mm at most, and release one drop. The drop will spread in a ring across the surface. Now load the dropper with distilled water and drop one water drop in the centre of the ink ring. The water drop pushes the ink ring outward. Add another ink drop in the centre. Another water drop. Continue alternating: ink, water, ink, water. Each drop pushes the previous ring outward, building the characteristic concentric pattern.

A gentle breath across the surface at any point will distort the rings into curves and spirals, this is the 'blow' technique. One breath per sheet produces a flowing variation; repeated blowing breaks the rings entirely into abstract shapes.

Hold the mordanted sheet at the short edges. Lower it face-down onto the water surface, starting at one end and lowering progressively to avoid trapping air. Let it rest for two seconds. Lift by one corner, peeling it off the water surface in one smooth motion. The ink lifts with the sheet. Rinse the sheet under a gentle cold-water tap to remove any surface scum, then lay flat to dry.

Suminagashi prints are stable once dry. Store flat in a portfolio away from humidity. The ink is not waterproof once printed, do not use as endpapers in a book that might be stored in damp conditions without first protecting the printed sheet with a dilute gelatin coating.

items.fix: Dilute the sumi ink with distilled water to reduce density. Rinse the tray thoroughly before each session, soap or any cleaning product residue destroys surface tension.
items.cause: The ink is too dense, or there is a surfactant residue in the tray that breaks the surface tension.
items.symptom: The ink sinks rather than spreading on the water surface

items.fix: Mordant the paper again with a more concentrated alum solution. The mordanted face must be the face that touches the water.
items.cause: The paper was not mordanted, or the alum was not properly distributed.
items.symptom: The pattern does not transfer to the paper, the print is blank
